entry.title: Setting Utilization % as low as 30-40% on some work centers to match real output — is this the right
entry.content:
System: SAP ECC 6.0, EHP8

Setting Utilization % as low as 30-40% on some work centers to match real output — is this the right use of the field?

We're standardizing how we set work center capacity across a multi-site manufacturing operation — eight to ten distinct processing types, hundreds of work centers company-wide and thousands of material masters -  I'm really struggling to accept the new approach and believe it should be challenged before it's locked in everywhere.

The directive coming down: set every work center's shift capacity at around 7 standard hours (based on an 8.5-hour shift, less standard breaks), the directive is to leave Length of Break at zero, and leave Cap. Utilization % at 100 — across the board, regardless of what a work center actually produces. There's a fairly firm belief behind this that setting utilization to anything other than 100% will cause problems with scheduling though I haven't seen that pinned down to anything specific yet. I firmly believe breaks and utilization % are fields to be used in CR12 and only affects available capacity which finite scheduling with fill and level against.

 

What I'm proposing instead: keep Start/End Time and breaks reflecting the real 8.5-hour shift schedule, and use Cap. Utilization % to scale capacity down to match each work center's demonstrated output — a rolling 30-60 day average of what it actually produces.

 

One methodology note: demonstrated output or historical confirmed std hours per day would be pulled from work centers and processing types that were fully loaded during that window — i.e., not idle or underutilized for other reasons — so the number reflects a real ceiling, not a slow period. That said, we know this won't land perfectly for every single work center as there are many variables that affect those work centers (machines) ability to output at the desired targets. It's meant as a defensible target to set capacity against and refine over time, not a one-time precise measurement.

 

Here's the part I expect will raise eyebrows: on some work centers, that means setting utilization as low as 30-40%. The gap between theoretical shift capacity and real output on certain machines is that large, driven by a mix of things — newer employees still ramping up, machine reliability issues, standard labor calculation errors either in formulas or MM specific std labor, general staffing levels at machines for loading and unloading, product mix and plain inefficiency. None of those are shift-length problems, so a flat number applied to every work center regardless of these differences doesn't fix any of them — it just hides them behind a ceiling that they are currently not or may never reach.

 

One more piece of context: we're also running a parallel initiative to improve actual labor capture at order confirmation as this has never been done to validate many labor estimates or old formulas. Up to now, actual hours haven't been recorded consistently — confirmations typically default to standard, which means we've had limited real data to separate a genuine performance gap from an inaccurate standard. That data is going to get better, but the capacity setup shouldn't wait on it — the output data we do have (from production reporting, independent of confirmation defaults) already shows the gap clearly enough to act on.

My questions for the community:

  1. Is using Utilization % this way — scaled per work center to align set capacity with demonstrated/capable output — the correct approach? Or is there a better mechanism intended for this?
  2. Is there a real technical basis for the belief that utilization below 100% causes problems? Or is that more of an organizational convention than an actual system constraint?

 

What I'm trying to protect: visibility of bottlenecks, a capacity that reflects what a work center can actually do, and orders that get produced and shipped on time. A standard capacity that very few work centers can hit defeats all three — it generates ATP dates against capacity that isn't there, and backlog builds invisibly until it's a missed delivery instead of an early warning.

 

Looking for real-world experience: has anyone set utilization this low on specific work centers, and did it hold up over time, especially across a large number of work centers and processing types? Any downsides I should know before challenging the current directive?

entry.title: SAC SMA Function not generating any value
entry.content:
We are able to create a Simple Moving Average (SMA) calculation in our SAP Analytics Cloud model without any errors. However, the calculation does not return any values.

The calculated measure is created successfully and no validation or runtime errors are displayed, but the result is always blank when used in the model or in a Story.

entry.title: Solution order revenue recognition
entry.content:
Hello experts.

We are implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition using the Solution Order scope item, and we have the following business scenario:

A sales order is created for the sale of an engine. At the same time, a service order is created to plan and execute the installation of that engine.

The sales order is processed completely through billing, resulting in the corresponding accounting document. Meanwhile, the service order is used to plan the work and record the consumption of materials.

The business requirement is that the service order should not be billed separately, and that all costs incurred in the service order should be assigned to the sales document.

However, the standard functionality does not allow this type of service order to be settled to a cost object. Therefore, we would like to understand if by linking both the sales order and the service order to the same Solution Order, the revenue from the sales document is automatically associated with the costs recorded in the service order for profitability and reporting purposes.

We have also reviewed the analytical apps for Solution Orders, but we do not see any revenue or costs being reported. Therefore, we are wondering whether an additional process in Controlling (CO) or Financial Accounting (FI) is required to make this information available.

We have completed the entire 4GT test script, with the exception of billing the service order, since the business requirement is that the service order must not generate revenue. It should only record costs, while the only revenue should come from the sales order.

 

Thank you in advanced.

Best regards.

entry.title: Re: S-users Manage Their Own Cloud Notification Subscript...
entry.content:
egarding the question: "Which cloud notification subscriptions are applicable to our current SAP landscape?"

Please refer to the responses below:
  1. The cloud Production email notifications your company is eligible to subscribe to depend on two criteria:

    • Whether your company holds a valid contract for the respective product. To view your contracts, follow the steps to navigate to Finance & Legal -> Cloud -> Contracts.
    • Whether the product supports subscriptions via the Get Notified application. For detailed information, please refer to KBA 3164317 – Which cloud products are available in the Manage Cloud Service Availability Notifications? – SAP for Me.

    There is also a simpler way to verify eligible products for subscription. Ask a Cloud Administrator with sufficient authorizations to access the Get Notified application. The cloud services visible for selection in line with the screenshot represent all cloud products your company can subscribe to.

  2. As for which cloud notification subscriptions should be enabled for our S-users, this is determined by your company’s operational requirements. As SAP Technical Support, we are unable to assess your business needs and provide recommendations. Please select the subscribed products based on your own requirements.

entry.title: From Stakeholder Alignment to Inventory Optimization: What’s New in SAP Signavio Value Accelerators
entry.content:
Successful transformation depends on more than identifying opportunities for improvement. Organizations also need the ability to align stakeholders, prioritize actions, and execute changes with confidence. This latest SAP Signavio release introduces new capabilities designed to support both sides of that equation: helping teams collaborate more effectively around value accelerator adoption while uncovering actionable opportunities to optimize inventory performance.

From evaluating and selecting the right value accelerators to identifying excess inventory and improving safety stock settings, these enhancements help organizations turn insights into measurable business outcomes. The new downloadable content overview enables administrators, process owners, and business stakeholders to collaborate more efficiently around deployment decisions, creating a shared understanding of available value accelerator content without requiring direct access to the system. At the same time, a new inventory optimization value accelerator helps supply chain teams gain visibility into potential excess stock, prolonged inventory coverage, and safety stock settings that may no longer reflect business needs.

Read on to explore what’s new for this July release:

Value Accelerator Library

Value Accelerator Content Overview Download

Adopting a value accelerator is often a collaborative decision that involves more than just system administrators. Process owners, business stakeholders, and transformation teams all need visibility into the available content before deciding what should be deployed. To support these discussions, SAP Signavio now provides a downloadable content overview directly from the value accelerators library.

This enhancement gives teams an easy way to share accurate and up-to-date information about a value accelerator beyond the confines of the application. Instead of relying on manually maintained documentation or screenshots that can quickly become outdated, administrators can generate a comprehensive overview document and distribute it to stakeholders for review. By making value accelerator content accessible offline, organizations can engage a broader audience in the evaluation process, including stakeholders who may not have direct access to the system. This enables more informed discussions, simplifies cross-functional collaboration, and helps teams align more quickly on which accelerators best support their transformation objectives.

The result is a smoother decision-making process with a single source of truth that can be easily shared across teams. By reducing the effort required to communicate and review accelerator content, organizations can move from evaluation to deployment faster while ensuring that all key stakeholders are aligned along the way.

Value Accelerator Content Overview Download

Supply Chain

Improvement Opportunity Dashboard to Reduce Excess Stock and Safety Stock Levels

Maintaining the right inventory levels is a constant balancing act. Excess stock ties up working capital and consumes valuable warehouse space, while inaccurate safety stock settings can obscure opportunities for optimization. To help organizations identify and address these challenges, SAP Signavio introduces a new value accelerator focused on inventory optimization and safety stock management.

Built for supply chain and inventory management teams, this accelerator provides a dedicated improvement opportunity dashboard in SAP Signavio Process Insights that highlights material and plant combinations with potentially excessive inventory levels or safety stock settings that may no longer reflect actual demand patterns.

The dashboard helps teams quickly identify inventory situations that warrant further investigation, including materials with exceptionally high stock coverage and prolonged days of supply. It also surfaces cases where stock levels fall below defined safety stock thresholds despite available inventory already covering replenishment lead times, revealing potential opportunities to reassess safety stock definitions and planning parameters.

Key improvement opportunities include:

  • Material and plant combinations with 999 days of supply and available stock
  • Material and plant combinations with more than 180 days of supply
  • Material and plant combinations where stock is below the defined safety stock level but stock coverage exceeds replenishment lead time
  • Material and plant combinations where stock exceeds the defined maximum stock level for reorder point planning materials

By translating inventory and planning data into actionable insights, the accelerator helps organizations identify excess stock, improve inventory policies, and make more informed planning decisions. The result is better alignment between inventory levels and actual business needs, enabling companies to reduce unnecessary stock, free up warehouse capacity, and release working capital that can be invested elsewhere in the business.

Improvement Opportunity Dashboard to Reduce Excess Stock and Safety Stock Levels

Conclusion

This latest SAP Signavio release not only helps organizations uncover new improvement opportunities but also makes it easier to collaborate on and act upon them. By introducing downloadable value accelerator content overviews and a new inventory optimization accelerator, SAP Signavio supports both better decision-making and more effective execution across transformation initiatives.

The downloadable content overview simplifies how teams evaluate and adopt value accelerators by providing a shareable, up-to-date source of information that can be reviewed by administrators, process owners, and business stakeholders alike. At the same time, the new inventory optimization accelerator helps supply chain teams identify excess stock, reassess safety stock settings, and uncover opportunities to free up working capital through data-driven insights.

Together, these enhancements reinforce SAP Signavio’s commitment to accelerating process transformation with practical, business-focused capabilities. Whether organizations are aligning stakeholders on accelerator adoption or identifying opportunities to optimize inventory performance, SAP Signavio continues to help teams move from insight to action faster, enabling more informed decisions, stronger cross-functional collaboration, and measurable business outcomes.

 

To check out all the new accelerators mentioned in this blog post, access the value accelerator library which is embedded into SAP Signavio Process Transformation Suite. For all SAP Signavio Process Insights value accelerators, you can access them directly through the dashboard. 

Find the value accelerator library in the side panel of SAP Signavio Process Collaboration Hub and get started today!

If you’re interested in learning more about the rest of the SAP Signavio solutions, check out the blog posts below:

If you’re interested in learning more about the rest of the SAP Signavio solutions, check out the blog posts below:

  • SAP Signavio July 2026 Release Highlights webcast
  • Process and Journey Modeling
  • AI-assisted process modeler
  • Data Driven Process Simulation

entry.title: Variable pay for Bonus calculation Equation custom formula
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am using a custom formula in the Basis Calculation Equation to calculate different basis amounts depending on whether an employee is on Leave of Absence (LOA), since the bonus calculation is prorated based on the employee's hire date and the time spent on LOA.

To support this requirement, I mapped the LOA information from a custom field in Job Information to Employee History. However, after making this change and trying to perform the Import Employee History from Employee Central, the job fails every time with the following error:

"This scheduled job failed to complete. For details, please contact Technical Support.."

To troubleshoot the issue, I performed the following tests:

  • If I keep the IF condition but replace the custom LOA field with a standard field (for example, Country), the job completes successfully, and Employee History is imported without any issues.

  • If I remove the IF condition and leave only a single calculation in the formula, the job also completes successfully.

Has anyone experienced similar behavior? Are there any known limitations when using custom Job Information fields in a Basis Calculation Equation, or is there any additional configuration required to make custom fields available in these formulas?

Any insights or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Financial Accounting
entry.content:
Business Scenario

As Bike Company, we serve multiple customers across various segments. However, we are 

aware that one particular customer has a history of delayed payments.

A dunning process should be in place for cases where invoices become overdue to ensure 

timely follow-up and improve receivables management. Should the payment deadline be 

missed, the system is configured so that the customer immediately receives a dunning notice 

at level 1 upon completion of the days of arrears period.

This automated process supports proactive receivables control while maintaining 

professional and consistent communication with our customers.

To implement this in the system, you need to perform the following steps:

1. Create a customer invoice:

Generate a customer invoice for customer S4C273, amounting to € 1,273, dated with 

today’s invoice date. The invoice should be set as due immediately. Please ensure that you 

use Company Code 1010, G/L Account 41000500, and Tax Code A0 for the posting.

After posting the document, make a note of the document number for reference.

2. Prepare and execute a dunning run for your customer S4C273:

Use an existing dunning procedure for your Business Partner S4C273 that applies a 

dunning charge of €10 at dunning level 1 on the dunning notice. Please don’t modify 

existing dunning procedures or create a new one.

When analyzing and selecting the dunning procedure, consider the following information 

for the subsequent dunning run:

● Check the number of days in arrears in your dunning procedure for dunning level 1. 

Then, execute the dunning run on the following date. After the dunning run, this date 

must be visible in the Last Dunning Date field in the business partner master record.

● Use the app Create Dunning Notices for your dunning run. Enter 273 as identification. 

Please restrict the dunning run exclusively to company code 1010, customer S4C273, 

and the invoice created in step 1. The date in the Documents Posted Up To Date field 

must match the dunning date.

Kindly help in configuring this scenario.

entry.title: Sales Order exceeds Credit Limit is NOT automatically blocked with Credit Management
entry.content:
When I created a Sales Order with value exceeds the Credit limit, the credit management does not block that Sales Order. 

Please see the step to Reproduce for further information

 

  1. Set update group 000015 for Segment 1000 (Open delivery and billing document value) – SSCUID: 103480. 
  2. Set update group 000012 for Segment 2000 (Open order value on time axis, delivery and billing document value).
  3. All other configurations remain the same between the two segments.
  4. Assign a Credit Limit = 1000 for both segments to a new customer.
  5. Create the first Sales Order with value = 1200 for both segments of that customer.

  • Sales Order A for Segment 2000 is blocked (expected – correct).

  • Sales Order B for Segment 1000 is not blocked (unexpected). It is only blocked at the Delivery creation step (correct).

My question: Please confirm whether Sales Order B, which is not blocked at creation, is standard behavior of the SAP system.

entry.title: Re: Parent child site in CDC
entry.content: This is the error: "ID Token does not match the payload hash." The call is going inside validateWebHookJWTToken to validate the JWT token. Then here we have 2 checks jwtValid = getGigyaLoginService().verifyGigyaCallIdTokenExpiryAndSignature(gigyaConfig, jwtToken) && getGigyaLoginService().verifyGigyaIdTokenContainsPayloadHash(jwtToken, payloadHash); This condition is failing: getGigyaLoginService().verifyGigyaIdTokenContainsPayloadHash(jwtToken, payloadHash); Inside this we have validateTokenPayloadHash: if (hashInJWT.equals(payloadHash)) { return true; } } Observed OOTB log:ID Token does not match the payload hash. Confirmed: JWT expiry passes. JWT RSA signature passes. Failure occurs only in validateTokenPayloadHash(). CDC JWT sub hash differs from Commerce generatePayloadHash(). Can someone help me here.

entry.title: Re: Parent child site in CDC
entry.content:
Hi @Ritumbhara 

Thanks for sharing the project issue.

Please correct me if I misunderstand the problem. The webhook is registered at the Parent site in SAP CDC. When the event fires, the JWT in the webhook request is signed using the Parent site's API key.
However, the SAP CDC site's API key in your SAP Commerce setup points to the child site.
The GConnector extensions fetch the JWT public key using the child site's API key — but the JWT was signed with the parent site's private key. The public key lookup and/or signature verification fails. 

Here is a quick fix to point the API key to the Parent site in gigyaConfig.
This way, the JWT signed by the parent site will be validated against the parent's public key. You only need one GigyaConfig linked to the parent site, configured with the parent site's API key.

entry.title: Re: Parent child site in CDC
entry.content: Hello Samuel, Thanks for responding but i think parent api key is not the issue as for that also we are getting the same issue. Because in the flow jwtValid = getGigyaLoginService().verifyGigyaCallIdTokenExpiryAndSignature(gigyaConfig, jwtToken) this is getting passed with child API key. The issue is coming in the next check getGigyaLoginService().verifyGigyaIdTokenContainsPayloadHash(jwtToken, payloadHash). In this the error is This is the error: "ID Token does not match the payload hash." The call is going inside validateWebHookJWTToken to validate the JWT token. Then here we have 2 checks jwtValid = getGigyaLoginService().verifyGigyaCallIdTokenExpiryAndSignature(gigyaConfig, jwtToken) && getGigyaLoginService().verifyGigyaIdTokenContainsPayloadHash(jwtToken, payloadHash); This condition is failing: getGigyaLoginService().verifyGigyaIdTokenContainsPayloadHash(jwtToken, payloadHash); Inside this we have validateTokenPayloadHash: if (hashInJWT.equals(payloadHash)) { return true; } }

entry.title: Request for guidance to support logistics billing pricing update through SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Ed
entry.content:
3. Business Requirement

In logistics, final freight and other transportation charges are often known only at invoice creation. Example: Sales Order ZFRT = INR 1,000 but final freight at billing may be INR 1,200 or INR 800. The Billing document should reflect the final value received from Movex.

4. Current Behaviour

When Billing is created with reference to the Sales Order, SAP copies pricing from the Sales Order using copy control. We could not find any released API or released BAdI in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition that allows replacing or updating copied pricing conditions during Billing creation.

5. Request to Experts 

  • Is there any released API to replace or update pricing conditions while creating the Billing document?
  • Is there any released BAdI or extensibility option for this requirement?
  • What is the SAP recommended standard solution for logistics businesses where final freight is known only during invoicing?
  • If this is not currently supported, what integration architecture or best practice does SAP recommend?

entry.title: SAC import connection: master data and property mapping from SuccessFactors
entry.content:
currently have SAC and SuccessFactors in scope, with only import connection. I can see master data APIs such as FOCOSTCENTER, FOCOMPANYCODE, FODIVISION, and FODEPARTMENT, but I could not find a master data API for the Position dimension.

I do see a Position table that includes measures and other dimensions, and it appears to contain the fields we need. My questions are:

  1. For Position master data and Employee, should we use the transactional data table and extract the required fields from that query to feed the master data?
  2. If we want to maintain mappings such as Department or Division as properties while loading Cost Center, how can that be achieved in SAC? 
  3. Since there is no middleware between SuccessFactors and SAC, and transformations must happen in SAC while creating the jobs, what is the recommended approach for handling this mapping logic?

https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-platform/sap-successfactors-api-reference-guide-odata-v2/mdf-foundation-object-entities?locale=en-US

Any guidance or best-practice examples would be appreciated.

Thanks

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 3 — Multiplayer & Events
entry.content:
👉🏻 The play by play at the end made a mistake. Do you know why?

1.  Multiplayer game started with players in different states.

2. Roll dice with MCP

3. Claude Code managing the game

4. Narrated play-by-play of last 5 moves -- I waited until someone hit ladder or snake to make it more exciting 🗣️ 🎙️

Love this!!! Except ... Turn 5, Alice is on 5 and rolls a ONE, but somehow she advances to square 7!!! How is that? Bob should play the game under protest. 

P.S.: The turn log is showing from space 5 to 7 on a roll of 2, which is correct, and is confirmed by the Claude Code panel which shows that on turn 5 the roll was 2. So why in the play by play it says she rolled a ONE again? All that I can imagine is that the LLM was told to make the play by play exciting, and it would be more exciting to think that Alice is trailing because she keeps rolling 1s, the worst roll there is.

entry.title: Re: Solution order revenue recognition
entry.content:
Hello DavidAvila,

This scenario should be possible with solution order and universal revenue recognition. With this, you can have a revenue contract for a solution order containing performance obligations of both sales item and service item and cross allocate the revenue from the service POBJ to the sales one. Please have a look at this blog https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/an-introduction-of-the-end-2-end-process-in-universal-revenue-recognition/ba-p/14333641 .  Best Regards, Pragya

entry.title: Export Selected Rows to Excel in SAP Fiori Elements Using Custom Actions
entry.content:
Hello Everyone, 

While working on a CAP-based SAP Fiori Elements application, I came across a requirement where users needed to export only the selected records from the List Report page instead of the entire dataset. 

SAP Fiori Elements provides a standard Export option that downloads all the records displayed in the table. However, there may be business scenarios where users need to export only specific rows that they have selected. 

In this blog, I'll show you how to create a custom action that exports only the selected rows to an Excel file using the sap.ui.export.Spreadsheet API. 

Prerequisites 

Before implementing this solution, ensure that: 

  • You have a CAP-based SAP Fiori Elements application.  
  • Your List Report page is already displaying data.  
  • You are using SAPUI5 version that supports sap.ui.export.Spreadsheet.  
  • You have created a Controller Extension for your List Report. 

Step 1: Create a Custom Action Using a Controller Extension 

Add a handler function in the controller extension and update the manifest.json file to register the custom action. 

In this step, you create a controller extension to define a handler function for the custom action button and add the extension settings of the custom action to the manifest.json file. 

If a controller extension is already available for a page, you can copy the code and use it. 

Add the following configuration to the manifest.json file: 

"controlConfiguration": {
                "@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.LineItem": {
                  "tableSettings": {
                    "type": "ResponsiveTable"
                  },
                  "actions": {
                    "controller1": {
                      "press": "project1.ext.controller.controller1.exportSelectedRowData",
                      "visible": true,
                      "enabled": true,
                      "requiresSelection": true,
                      "text": "Export Selected Rows of Data"
                    }
                  }
                }
              }

Step 2: Implement the Export Logic in the Controller Extension 

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/m/MessageToast",
    "sap/ui/export/Spreadsheet",
    "sap/ui/export/library"
], function(MessageToast, Spreadsheet, exportLibrary) {
    'use strict';
    const EdmType = exportLibrary.EdmType;
    return {
        /**
         * Generated event handler.
         *
         * @Param oContext the context of the page on which the event was fired. `undefined` for list report page.
         * @Param aSelectedContexts the selected contexts of the table rows.
         */
        exportSelectedRowData:function(oContext, aSelectedContexts) {
            // 1. Guard clause: Ensure there are selected rows
            if (!aSelectedContexts || aSelectedContexts.length === 0) {
                MessageToast.show("Please select at least one row to export.");
                return;
            }

            // 2. Extract the raw JSON data from the selected contexts
            const aSelectedData = aSelectedContexts.map(function(oRowContext) {
                return oRowContext.getObject();
            });

            // 3. Define the columns for the Excel file
            const aColumns = [
                {
                    label: 'Department ID',
                    property: 'ID',    
                    type: EdmType.String             
                },
                {
                    label: 'Department Name',
                    property: 'deptName',
                    type: EdmType.String
                },
                {
                    label: 'Location',
                    property: 'location',
                    type: EdmType.String
                }
            ];

            // 4. Configure the Spreadsheet settings
            const mSettings = {
                workbook: {
                    columns: aColumns,
                    context: {
                        sheetName: 'Selected Row Data Sheet'
                    }
                },
                dataSource: aSelectedData,
                fileName: 'Selected_Departments.xlsx',
                worker: false // Set to false because we are passing client-side JSON data directly
            };

            // 5. Generate and download the Excel file
            const oSheet = new Spreadsheet(mSettings);
            
            oSheet.build()
                .then(function() {
                    MessageToast.show("Exported selected rows successfully.");
                })
                .catch(function(sMessage) {
                    MessageToast.show("Export failed: " + sMessage);
                })
                .finally(function() {
                    // Always destroy the spreadsheet instance to prevent memory leaks
                    oSheet.destroy();
                });
        }
    };
});

Now let's understand how the export functionality works internally 

Step 1: Import the Required Libraries 

Import the required SAPUI5 libraries for Excel export. 

  • sap/ui/export/Spreadsheet – Used to create and generate Excel files.  
  • sap/ui/export/library – Provides export-related data types such as EdmType, which are used while defining the Excel columns.  

Step 2: User Selects Rows 

The user selects one or more rows from the List Report table and clicks the Export Selected Rows of Data button. This action triggers the exportSelectedRowData() function. 

Step 3: Validate the Selection 

The application checks whether at least one row has been selected. If no rows are selected, a message is displayed to the user, and the export process is stopped. 

Step 4: Convert Context Objects to JSON 

The selected rows are received as binding context objects. Using the getObject() method, each context is converted into a plain JavaScript object. These objects are then stored in an array, which serves as the data source for the Excel export. 

Step 5: Define the Excel Columns 

The application defines the structure of the Excel file by specifying the column headers (label), the corresponding data properties (property), and the data type (EdmType). 

Step 6: Configure the Spreadsheet 

A Spreadsheet configuration object is created by specifying the workbook details, sheet name, column definitions, data source, and output file name. These settings determine how the Excel file will be generated. 

Step 7: Generate the Excel File 

A new Spreadsheet instance is created using the configuration object. The build() method then generates the Excel workbook and automatically downloads it to the user's system. 

Step 8: Handle Success or Errors 

The application displays a success message if the export is completed successfully. If an error occurs during the export process, an appropriate error message is displayed. 

Step 9: Destroy the Spreadsheet Object 

Finally, the Spreadsheet object is destroyed to release the resources occupied by it and prevent memory leaks. 

Output 

Selecting the Required Rows 

The user selects one or more rows from the List Report and clicks the Export Selected Rows of Data button. 

Excel Export in Progress 

After clicking the export button, the Spreadsheet API generates the Excel document. Once the process is completed, a success message is displayed. 

 Downloaded Excel File 

The generated Excel file is automatically downloaded to the user's local system with the configured file name (Selected_Departments.xlsx). 

Conclusion 

In this blog, we learned how to implement a custom action in SAP Fiori Elements that exports only the selected rows from the List Report into an Excel file. 

Unlike the standard Export functionality, which exports all records, this approach gives users greater flexibility by allowing them to download only the data they need. 

Since the implementation uses the sap.ui.export.Spreadsheet API, it can be easily reused across different SAP Fiori Elements applications with minimal changes to the column configuration. 

Thank you for reading! I hope you found this blog helpful and that it helps you implement a similar requirement in your own projects.

Happy Coding!

entry.title: Real-Time Safety Governance: Automating NFPA Fire Water Demand Calculation with SAP EHS, EAM & MoC
entry.content:
The Compliance Gap: Where Engineering Theory Meets Operational Reality

Hi All !

I am writing this blog post on Fire Water Demand Calculation in fire protection system design mapping in SAP with EH&S, EAM and SAP Management of Change.

In high-hazard process industries—such as chemicals, oil & gas, pharmaceuticals, and heavy manufacturing—Fire Water Demand Calculation is not just a checkbox exercise; it is an foundational safety baseline. Dictated by standards like NFPA 13 and NFPA 24, these calculations determine the exact volumetric flow rates (Q = D x A) and storage volumes (V = Q x t) required to suppress a catastrophic event.

Historically, this critical engineering data has been trapped in siloed calculation sheets, third-party hydraulic software, or static PDFs.

The risk isn't the initial math—it’s the delta created over time by operational evolution.

Let us consider this common scenario: A production line is reconfigured, or a warehouse space is modified to hold raw chemical drums instead of finished boxed goods. The occupancy profile instantly escalates from an Ordinary Hazard to an Extra Hazard rating. In a legacy paradigm, the physical modification happens weeks or months before a manual engineering review flags that the existing fire pumps and onsite water storage tanks are now significantly under-sized.

By building an integrated, closed-loop solution directly inside the SAP S/4HANA Digital Core, we can bridge this gap between engineering compliance and active asset management.

Architectural Blueprints: The Closed-Loop Ecosystem

To turn the static NFPA standards into an automated process safety gate, we map the requirements across the four interconnected SAP modules and engine components, shifting the ownership from rigid modules to collaborative business roles:

+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                         SAP S/4HANA DIGITAL SAFETY CORE                         |
|                                                                                 |
|  [1. Production Manager] ---> Triggers Change Request via SAP MoC               |
|                                     |                                           |
|                                     v                                           |
|  [2. EHS Manager] ------------> Evaluates NFPA Hazard Classes & Run Audits      |
|                                     |                                           |
|                                     v                                           |
|  [3. BRFplus Rules Architect] -> Executes Dynamic Calculation: Q = Density * Area|
|                                     |                                           |
|                                     v                                           |
|  [4. Asset Manager] -----------> Validates Live Pump/Tank Master Capacities     |
|                                                                                 |
+---------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

1. SAP Management of Change (MoC): The Governance Gatekeeper

Managed by the Production Manager (Process Initiator), any structural change, expansion, or material throughput increase must initiate a Change Request (MOC REQ). The workflow acts as an automated block on the physical asset's status, ensuring that operational variations cannot bypass engineering review.

2. SAP EHS Risk Assessment & Compliance Management

Overseen by the EHS Manager (Safety Compliance Leader), the specific asset location (Functional Location) is evaluated for localized hazards. If a material classification changes, the system flags the risk profile. Concurrently, SAP EHS Audit & Inspection Management enables field safety auditors to execute mobile checklists. If a routine test uncovers a degraded fire hydrant loop or an impaired deluge valve, this physical asset variance is captured immediately.

3. SAP S/4HANA BRFplus (Business Rule Framework plus)

The BRFplus Rules Architect (Safety Rules Owner / administrator) maintains standard NFPA density curves and design area matrices inside BRFplus decision tables. Rather than manually pulling variables, the system feeds the live variables from EHS into the standard hydraulic formulas:

Q_sprinkler = Density (D) x Design Area (A)
Q_total = Q_sprinkler + Hose Stream Allowance

4. SAP Enterprise Asset Management (EAM)

The Asset Manager (Hydraulic Design Authority) oversees the technical attributes of physical assets (EQUI and IFLOT tables). The newly calculated Q_total and required storage volume V are dynamically compared against the actual performance metrics of the assigned fire pumps and storage tanks.

If a deficit is detected (e.g., the system requires 45,000 gallons of dedicated storage but the EAM asset record indicates a tank capacity of only 30,000 gallons), SAP MoC locks the change request. The process cannot advance to approval until a Plant Maintenance (PM) order or CAPEX engineering project is triggered to resolve the infrastructure deficit.

 

Designing the Target State: The BPMN 2.0 Role-Based Workflow

To implement this structure effectively, our process architecture should follow a role-based matrix rather than system boundaries. This ensures clear data ownership and smooth operational transitions. Below is the mapped target state layout developed using an SAP Signavio structure.

 

Key Integration Highlights of the Workflow:

  • The Automated Trigger: The process moves seamlessly from an operational modification to an automated risk evaluation step without relying on manual notifications.

  • The Discrepancy Gate: The exclusive gateway "Is Capacity Sufficient " leverages core asset master data to enforce safety limits before physical changes can occur on the shop floor.

  • Continuous Field Verification: Field-level inputs from the Safety Auditor are integrated directly into the verification loop, ensuring the asset profile reflects real-world capabilities rather than purely theoretical values.

Integrated Step-by-Step Process Mapping

To realize a modern digital safe-state, the traditional static NFPA infographic steps are converted into active operational processes that dynamically engage SAP S/4HANA modules, now inclusive of continuous verification field layers:

 

NFPA Step

Technical Description

SAP S/4HANA Module Component

SAP Data Entity / Object Type

1. Hazard Classification

Classifying occupancy risk profile based on local combustibility, storage heights, and fuel types.

SAP EHS (Risk Assessment & Industrial Hygiene)

Risk Assessment Log, Business Partner (BP), Risk Agents

2. Density & Design Area

Deriving mandated water distribution rates (gpm/ft²) and operational spaces via NFPA tables.

SAP S/4HANA Core (BRFplus Rules)

BRFplus Expression, Regulatory Matrix Objects

3. Sprinkler Flow Calculation

Processing formula Q = D × A to determine base volumetric flow at hydraulically remote nodes.

SAP BRFplus Engine / Core ABAP

Calculated Expression Field, Linear Data Asset Records

4. Hose Stream Allowance

Adding mandatory supplementary flow values required for firefighting hoses.

SAP EHS (Compliance Mgmt) & EAM

Safety Requirement Objects, Equipment Attribute Fields

5. Total Fire Water Demand

Summing calculation steps into total volumetric system throughput: Q_total = Q_sprinkler + Q_hose.

SAP S/4HANA Core Analytics

Core CDS Views, Fiori Analytical Cards

6. Storage / Sizing Sizing

Determining necessary storage capacities using duration rules (V = Q_total × t).

SAP EAM (Asset Sizing & Strategy)

Equipment Master (Tanks), Maintenance Strategy Tables

7. Continuous Audit & Inspection Verification

Regular field inspections of hydrants, physical infrastructure loops, and asset health profiles.

SAP EHS Audit & Inspection Management

Audit Plan, Inspection Checklist, Corrective & Preventive Action (CAPA)

8. Change Governance

Controlling variations in plant geometry, structural loads, or water capabilities.

SAP MoC (Management of Change)

Change Request (MOC_REQ), Workflow Task Engine

Business Value and Resilience Factors

Integrating the safety engineering logic directly with our central enterprise data core unlocks significant performance advantages:

  • Eradication of Hidden Risk Windows: Eliminates the vulnerability window where real-world operational changes temporarily outpace manual safety engineering logs.

  • Uncompressed Audit Trails: Provides an unalterable, time-stamped history tracking why a hazard class changed, how the system processed the math, and how the infrastructure was validated. This streamlines compliance audits for regulatory agencies and insurance underwriters.

  • Optimized Fixed Capital Expenditures: Prevents over-engineering system specs out of uncertainty. By relying on exact data fields and embedded NFPA curves, safety system capacity investments match real-world requirements.

Future Horizons: Injecting SAP Business AI

By consolidating our safety calculations, asset logs, and inspection checks into a single data model, you build the foundation needed to leverage next-generation capabilities via SAP Business AI and Joule:

  1. Predictive Safety Adjustments: Machine learning models can analyze production schedules and real-time material flows to proactively flag areas approaching an engineering threshold, allowing teams to upgrade systems ahead of time.

  2. Computer Vision-Driven Validation: Deep learning models processing live edge camera feeds can analyze actual warehouse storage heights and stack layouts. If physical storage breaches the maximum height variables coded in SAP EHS, the system automatically triggers a corrective task in SAP EAM.

  3. Smart Hydraulic Health Diagnostics: By cross-referencing real-time Industrial IoT telemetry (active pipeline pressures and flow velocities) with the baseline demands stored in the ERP, generative AI can predict pipeline scaling, internal corrosion, or pump wear long before an emergency occurs.

How is your organization managing the intersection of engineering compliance and asset lifecycles?

Have you automated your safety thresholds within the digital core, or are you still relying on manual calculation sheets? Let's discuss your engineering validation strategies, asset tracking methods, and process experiences in the comments section below!

Kind Regards,

Hanish

SAP Audit Management NW ABAP Business Rule Framework (BRFplus) SAP Environment, Health, and Safety Management #MoC

entry.title: Re: VAT settlement in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition wi...
entry.content:
Hi @6-Mar 

Short answer: No single standard app outputs the official VAT settlement figure other than the VAT Return itself — for Portugal that net figure is tax box 93 (payable) or 94 (credit), produced only by the PT_VAT_DCL run. But you can validate the position beforehand with Tax Declaration Reconciliation, Tax Reconciliation Account Balance, and G/L balance apps, and you can also review a DRC run without submitting it.

Is there a standard app that gives the official settlement without running the statutory report?

No. The legal net payable/recoverable is calculated only by the VAT Return in SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (Run Statutory Reports). For Portugal, the calculation puts tax payable in box 93 (Imposto a entregar ao estado) and a tax credit in box 94 (crédito a recuperar), with 95/96 for refund vs. carry-forward. Reference: Periodic VAT Return and Annexes – Portugal (SAP Help). The apps below are for validation prior to reporting, not for legal filing.

Tax Declaration Reconciliation (App F2096) — closest to a return preview

It shows the tax-relevant postings to be declared in the VAT return and compares the posted tax amount against an app-calculated tax amount, with drill-down to the individual journal entry. SAP states explicitly it is "not intended to be used for reporting purposes" and should be used to identify issues before you report. Reference: Tax Declaration Reconciliation – SAP Help.

Constraint: it works cleanly only for "simple" tax-box mappings (a box maps to one tax group). For "complex"/enhanced tax-box structures — which the Portuguese return, with its transaction-type grouping and correction boxes 40/41, can fall under — the app may show "X" instead of the box values. In that case SAP's own guidance is to use the Analyze Data function in the Run Statutory Reports app instead.

Tax Reconciliation Account Balance (App F2095)

For consistency — not for the settlement figure. It verifies that tax-relevant G/L accounts are posted with tax attributes and that app-calculated VAT matches the posted amounts, letting you compare the Tax Base Amount against the Account Balance and isolate documents posted without a tax code. Differences are typically rounding. Same "not for reporting purposes" caveat applies. Reference: Tax Reconciliation Account Balance – SAP Help.

G/L Account Balances / Trial Balance

The most direct way to see a net number. Run Display G/L Account Balances (or Trial Balance / Display Financial Statement) on your output-VAT (payable) and input-VAT (deductible) reconciliation accounts for the period; output minus input gives an indicative net position. Accuracy depends entirely on your account determination, so treat it as a cross-check, not the legal value.

Reviewing the DRC run without submitting

Worth noting: generating a run in Run Statutory Reports does not send anything to the authority. You can open the generated run, review the values (Table View), and use Analyze Data for an analytical view of the amounts per box; submission is a separate, manual step. So a draft run is itself a safe way to see the full computed position before filing. This is the recommended way to analyze your data. Reference: VAT Return processing steps – SAP Help.

Other country versions

The three validation apps are country-agnostic (subject to the exclusion lists above), and the "draft run + Analyze Data" approach works for every DRC-enabled country, so the same method applies beyond Portugal.

Summary

For a quick net figure, use Display G/L Account Balances on your input/output VAT accounts. For box-level validation that mirrors the return, use Tax Declaration Reconciliation — and for Portugal, if the enhanced tax-box structure blanks the boxes, fall back to Analyze Data in Run Statutory Reports. Use Tax Reconciliation Account Balance to confirm postings are clean. None replace the legal return, but generating a DRC draft run (without submitting) is the safest way to see the exact settlement amount beforehand.

Hope it helps.
Best regards,
Michal

entry.title: How can I deploy an SAP RAP application for third-party users without providing SAP credentials?
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I have developed an application using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) on SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

My requirement is to make this application accessible to third-party users (such as vendors or customers) without creating SAP user accounts or sharing SAP credentials.

For example, I want to build a vendor portal where external vendors can:

  • View RFQs assigned to them.
  • Submit quotations.
  • Update the status of their responses.

The business users do not want external users to log in directly to SAP.

I would like to understand the recommended approach for this scenario:

  1. What is the best architecture for exposing a RAP application to external users?
  2. Can SAP Build or SAP BTP be used as the frontend while RAP remains the backend?
  3. Should I expose RAP business objects using OData/REST APIs and implement authentication using SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) or OAuth?
  4. Are there any SAP-recommended best practices or reference architectures for this use case?
  5. How are security, licensing, and authorization typically handled for external users?

If anyone has implemented a similar solution or can share documentation or architecture diagrams, I would greatly appreciate your guidance.

Thank you!
Omkar

entry.title: SAP Commerce Cloud Decoupled Hosting Service for JavaScript Storefronts
entry.content:
Why a New Hosting Service?

The new hosting service was introduced based on evolving customer needs and improvement requests. It focuses on helping organizations modernize frontend deployments while improving operational efficiency and storefront performance.

Some of the key objectives include:

  • Separating frontend build and deployment from the backend
  • Speeding up build and deployment processes
  • Supporting deployment variables
  • Improving storefront performance
  • Lowering the total cost of ownership (TCO)
  • Enabling faster feature delivery

Key Benefits

The new hosting service provides value to both business and development teams.

Faster Development

  • Frontend build and deployment are decoupled from the backend.
  • Teams can iterate more quickly without backend constraints.
  • Faster campaign and feature rollouts improve agility.

Improved Storefront Performance

  • Integrated CDN improves global UI response times.
  • Faster storefronts enhance customer experience.
  • Improved performance contributes to better SEO.

Lower Operational Overhead

  • Managed hosting reduces infrastructure management effort.
  • Built-in elasticity supports traffic spikes.
  • CDN helps reduce backend load during peak periods.

Solution Overview

The new hosting service consists of several integrated components working together to provide a complete hosting solution for JavaScript storefronts.

The solution includes:

  • JavaScript Application Server
  • SAP BTP Orchestration
  • Content Delivery Network (CDN)
  • Optional Backend-for-Frontend (BFF)

Together, these components enable simplified deployment, improved scalability, and better storefront performance.

Solution Architecture

At a high level, shopper requests are served through the CDN before reaching the JavaScript Application Server. The hosting environment is orchestrated through SAP BTP, while business data continues to come from SAP Commerce Cloud and connected backend systems.

The optional Backend-for-Frontend provides an additional layer for custom business logic and API aggregation.

The presentation illustrates two deployment scenarios:

Scenario 1

The storefront communicates directly with the JavaScript Application Server through the CDN.

Scenario 2

The storefront uses the optional Backend-for-Frontend before communicating with SAP Commerce Cloud and external systems.

These scenarios demonstrate the flexibility provided by the new hosting architecture while allowing customers to choose the approach that best fits their implementation needs.

SAP BTP Orchestration

One of the most important aspects of the new hosting service is that it is orchestrated through SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).

Instead of managing storefront hosting directly through SAP Commerce Cloud, SAP BTP provides the orchestration layer responsible for deployment and lifecycle management of the JavaScript storefront application.

This architecture helps separate frontend operations from the commerce backend while providing a streamlined deployment experience.

Key Capabilities

  • Orchestrated through SAP BTP
  • Simplified deployment management
  • Integration with Git-based workflows
  • Consistent deployment experience

Benefits

  • Simplifies storefront deployment
  • Decouples frontend lifecycle from backend
  • Provides a scalable deployment foundation
  • Improves operational efficiency

JavaScript Application Server

The JavaScript Application Server is responsible for serving the storefront HTML and JavaScript to shoppers.

It represents the evolution of the current JavaScript hosting service while introducing several new capabilities.

Features

  • Server-Side Rendering (SSR)
  • Custom domain support
  • Deployment variables
  • Independent frontend deployment
  • Azure-hosted infrastructure

Benefits

  • Faster frontend deployment
  • Better separation from backend
  • Improved developer experience
  • Better SEO support through SSR

Deployment Workflow

The deployment process has been simplified to enable faster storefront provisioning.

The high-level workflow includes:

  1. Create the storefront hosting source.
  2. Create the deployment.
  3. Wait for deployment to complete.
  4. Storefront becomes available.

Git-Based Build System

The hosting service introduces a Git-based development workflow where builds are automatically triggered after code changes are committed.

Highlights

  • Separation of frontend builds from Commerce backend
  • Automatic build triggering
  • Deployment variable support
  • Domain mapping support

This simplifies development while reducing dependencies on backend deployment cycles.

Deployment Variables

Deployment variables simplify application configuration across environments by allowing deployment-specific values to be managed separately from the application code.

This provides greater flexibility when deploying storefront applications.

Domain Mapping

The hosting service supports custom domain mapping, enabling storefront deployments to be associated with organization-specific domains.

This simplifies storefront configuration while providing greater deployment flexibility.

Backend-for-Frontend (BFF)

The optional Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) introduces a dedicated server layer between the storefront and backend systems.

Its purpose is to simplify frontend development while enabling additional customization scenarios.

Features

  • Aggregate multiple API calls
  • Off-load non-UI logic
  • Simplify frontend development
  • Reuse business logic

Benefits

  • Faster page rendering
  • Easier integration with multiple systems
  • Enhanced security
  • Improved flexibility for customizations

Note: As highlighted in the presentation, the current Composable Storefront does not require the BFF. Customers may use it for their own customization scenarios.

CDN Integration

The hosting service includes integrated CDN support to improve content delivery and storefront responsiveness.

Benefits

  • Faster global content delivery
  • Reduced backend load
  • Better reliability during traffic spikes
  • Improved customer experience

Conclusion

The new SAP Commerce Cloud Hosting Service for JavaScript Storefronts represents an important step toward modernizing storefront hosting.

By combining a JavaScript Application Server, SAP BTP orchestration, integrated CDN support, and an optional Backend-for-Frontend, the solution simplifies deployment while improving scalability, performance, and developer productivity.

For organizations adopting SAP Composable Storefront, this hosting service provides a modern foundation for delivering fast, scalable, and resilient digital commerce experiences.

entry.title: Re: 待機系の/hana/data領域の使用率増加要因について
entry.content:
Dear customer, I'm not sure about your HANA version, but I think you can try to get the current data volume status by running the following 2 scripts first:

1.Script: HANA_Disks_Data_Overview_2.00.030+.txt 

2.Script: HANA_Tables_TopGrowingTables_Size_History_2.00.060+.txt
This will help you to confirm whether it happened due to the table size increasing or the data volume fragmentation.

If it's related to data volume fragmentation, you can do the reclaim according to 2499913.

 2499913 - How to shrink SAP HANA Data volume size

Hope this can be helpful for you.

Have a nice day.

Best regards, 

Jane

entry.title: VAT Reporting in SAP FI — Tax Code Design, OB40 Account Determination & Down Payment Handling
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

Here's a common VAT reporting topic worth discussing for FI consultants  there are a few configuration and process decisions that seem to trip people up repeatedly, especially for those newer to tax reporting in SAP.

A few areas that come up often:

  1. Tax code design (FTXP) — using separate tax codes per scenario (standard rate, zero-rated, exempt, reverse charge) versus trying to consolidate codes and differentiate via account keys. Cleaner tax codes make reconciliation and VAT return prep much easier at period-end.
  2. Tax account determination (OB40) — getting the GL account assignment right per tax code and transaction key (e.g., VST for input tax, MWS for output tax) so postings land in the correct accounts automatically without manual correction.
  3. Down payments and VAT — a common pain point: handling VAT correctly on customer/vendor down payments (F-29, F-48) since the VAT treatment can differ from standard invoices depending on local tax law.

entry.title: Re: Question regarding IAS Group Filtering and Authorizat...
entry.content: Hi Matthias, Thank you for your input on my scenario and for sharing your experience working with SAP Cloud Identity Services (CIS). That is a great achievement. My main concern is that we want to configure Microsoft Entra ID as the Corporate Identity Provider for all our SAP applications and solutions to provide SSO. In this scenario, authentication should be successful through Entra. My question is about applications that are currently configured so that, after the user authenticates with Entra, Entra sends the basic user attributes as well as the groups assigned to that user. The target application then validates those group values and grants the appropriate access based on them. Is there a way to restrict or filter which groups are forwarded to the respective application when IAS/CIS is only acting as a proxy or broker, and the user identity does not exist in the IAS/CIS Identity Directory? Is my understanding correct, or can the same type of filtering also be applied in this proxy/broker scenario? Please let me know if I've misunderstood anything. Thank you!

entry.title: Fiori Cross Application Navigation to VL10C Opens Transaction Successfully but Does Not Prefill
entry.content:
A custom SAP Fiori application uses sap.ushell.services.CrossApplicationNavigation to launch VL10C/VL10F. Parameters VBELN (mapped to ST_VBELN-LOW), sap-ach=LE-SHP-GF, P_LERUL=2, and DYNP_OKCODE==S0S_TAB2 are passed through FLP target mapping.

The parameters are visible in the URL and navigation is successful; however, the Sales Order/Purchase Order tab is not selected by default, document fields are not populated, and the system displays "The entered selections were not applied".

  • Does SAP Fiori Launchpad intent-based navigation (Cross Application Navigation) support passing multiple values for parameter VBELN when launching WebGUI transactions such as VL10C and VL10F? If supported, what is the recommended approach for passing multiple document numbers?

  • If multiple values can be passed successfully, how can these values be automatically applied to the target transaction selection screen and populated in the corresponding fields (SD Document in VL10C and Purchasing Document in VL10F)?

  • Is there any specific configuration or supported mechanism to ensure that the Sales Orders tab in VL10C and the Purchase Orders tab in VL10F are automatically selected during launch, instead of opening on the default tab?

entry.title: Re: Unwanted change log is showing in business Partner
entry.content:
If the system is on S/4HANA, it is recommended to use CL_MD_BP_MAINTAIN or CVI_EI_ADAPTER (available in SE24) to update BP/vendor data instead of VMD_EI_API, as BP is the leading interface in S/4HANA.

If change logs are being created for address fields, this could be the scenario described in KBA 3638775 — implementing the notes mentioned in the KBA may resolve this.

Additionally, I would recommend testing whether the additional change logs are generated when updating SAP standard fields using CL_MD_BP_MAINTAIN or CVI_EI_ADAPTER. If the issue can be reproduced, please feel free to create a case under the BP master data area and we will be happy to investigate further in the system.

entry.title: Skin in the robotic game
entry.content:
The effort to create humanoid robots does not stop at the mechanical re-engineering of human mechanics. With natural language capabilities rapidly evolving, computers can engage in lively conversations which use – if they are online – the capabilities of resources like large language models. Think talking to OpenAI's GPT-Realtime via a humanoid robot.

While this is easy to explain, it is as well observable that the interactions with (humanoid) robots of today do not make use of these capabilities – but I have only seen a few, and the legal frameworks governing the access to these tools will certainly have an impact.

Voice interaction is a good example of the convergence of technology. Another striking example is that companies are bringing skin into the game: robots with faces and skin which feels soft to the touch.

As I've been engaged with robots and humanoids for some time now, I may be through what is called the "uncanny valley" already, and I would love to hear what your first reaction is when you look at the video I add to this blog post.

The uncanny valley is the uncomfortable feeling associated with machines becoming awkwardly human. While criticism is voiced over the exact curve of the valley, there is unanimous agreement that robots which become "too human" but are still identifiable as robots cause discomfort. My personal experience is that this goes away once you clearly tag them as robots and don't get caught in the initial zone of not being sure whether it's a human or a machine anymore.

 

When it comes to business, my first thought is that equipping robots with real skin (and up-to-speed voice interaction) is an amazing opportunity for gaming and entertainment parks. Imagine walking into a venue like a museum, where you could get to a new level of interactivity with historical characters – or simply create engaging role play scenarios (LARP) with non-player characters.

I'm focusing on gaming here because my personal perspective is that putting "realistic" robots in front of humans for interactions around their personal life is a real danger. Already today, people engage with computers as if they were talking to humans, and they did so since ELIZA. Adding skin to the game will further increase the risk of not being able to distinguish whether you engage with a human or not – and that risk is not that you treat a robot like you might have treated your favorite stuffed toy as a child. The risk is that this stuffed toy has access to almost unlimited information, might be able to analyze you better than any human, and may have been programmed (or: prompted) unknowingly to you to pursue an influencing agenda which you are not aware of.

That's why I put gaming forward, where the roles are clear – but let me know in the comments where you would see lifelike, skinned robots in application?

entry.title: Re: Need APP to lookup component quantity under exist pro...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

SAP App for Component Quantity Lookup

Unfortunately, there is no specific app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition that directly fulfills the requirement to lookup the required component quantity under multiple highest-level products as described in your example.

Alternative Solutions

  1. Material Where-Used List for BOMs: R&D / Engineering - Material Where-Used List for BOMs This app allows you to view where a material is used in BOMs, but it does not aggregate component quantities across multiple products.

  2. Explode Bills of Material: R&D / Engineering - Explode Bills of Material This app can display the overall structure of a BOM and view all components, but it does not provide a direct way to sum component quantities across different products.

  3. Manage Multilevel Bill of Material: R&D / Engineering - Manage Multilevel Bill of Material This app allows you to view and maintain the hierarchical tree structure of a multilevel BOM, but similar to the others, it does not aggregate component quantities across multiple BOMs.

Custom Development

For your specific requirement, you might need to consider custom development or enhancement using SAP’s extensibility options to create a custom report or app that can aggregate component quantities across multiple BOMs.

Here's how you could use custom development for this specific requirement:

Solution for Component Quantity Lookup

To achieve the requirement of looking up the required component quantity under multiple highest-level products, you can consider using the Custom CDS Views and Custom Analytical Queries in SAP S/4HANA Cloud. This approach allows you to create a tailored report that can aggregate component quantities across different products.

Steps to Implement:

  1. Create Custom CDS Views: Powerful way to create a CDS view based report on … - SAP Community

    • Use the ‘Custom CDS Views’ app to create a view that fetches data related to BOM components and their quantities.
    • Select the primary data source as I_BillOfMaterialItemDEX_2 and associate it with I_Product to join BOM components with product details.

    Create Custom Analytical Query:

    • Use the ‘Custom Analytical Queries’ app to create a query based on the CDS view created.
    • Define the query to aggregate component quantities for each product.

    Create Fiori Tile:

    • Create a Fiori tile for the query and assign it to the relevant business catalog.
  2. This custom development approach using CDS views and analytical queries will allow you to fulfill the requirement of aggregating component quantities across multiple products efficiently.

     

Note:

  • Ensure you have the necessary authorizations and roles assigned to access these apps.
  • This solution leverages the flexibility of SAP S/4HANA Cloud’s extensibility features to meet specific business needs.
Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Question regarding IAS Group Filtering and Authorizat...
